content.summary: Static analysis means examining a file's structure and content without running it — keeping you safe while uncovering what the software can do.
content.explanation: Static analysis is the practice of reading a program's bytes, structure, and metadata without ever executing the code. Unlike dynamic analysis (running the file in a sandbox), static analysis carries essentially zero risk of infection. Anya reads the file's headers, imports, strings, and entropy — none of which requires execution. The trade-off: static analysis cannot observe behaviour that only manifests at runtime, such as decryption of an encrypted payload or network communication. Think of it as reading a recipe rather than tasting the dish.
content.what_to_look_for: Start with the Overview tab: look at the risk score, the file format, and whether security mitigations (ASLR, DEP) are enabled. Then move to Imports — the import table tells you what the file *can* do without running it.
content.real_world_example: When WannaCry was discovered in 2017, static analysis immediately revealed its import of the EternalBlue exploit DLL and its ransomware-typical file enumeration and encryption APIs — all without running it on a single machine.
content.next_action: Drop a file onto Anya and look at the Overview tab. Note the risk score and the file format before exploring the other tabs.

glossary.definition: Examining a program without executing it.
Running a program in a controlled environment to observe its behaviour.
A list of external functions a program calls from other DLLs or shared libraries.

content.summary: PE (Portable Executable) is the file format used for .exe, .dll, and .sys files on Windows. It organises code, data, and metadata into sections with a structured header.
content.explanation: Every Windows executable follows the PE format: a DOS header (legacy), a PE signature, a COFF header describing the machine type and section count, an optional header with the entry point and security flags, a section table describing each chunk of the file, and then the section data itself. The most important sections are .text (code), .data (initialised globals), .rdata (read-only data, including the import/export tables), and .rsrc (resources). Malware often uses unusual section names, and packers frequently replace all sections with a stub loader and an encrypted payload.
content.what_to_look_for: Check the Sections tab. Look at section names, sizes, and entropy. Normal .text sections have moderate entropy (4–6). Entropy above 7.5 in an executable section usually means encrypted or packed code.
content.real_world_example: UPX-packed malware typically has two sections named 'UPX0' and 'UPX1' — instantly recognisable even before any dynamic analysis.
content.next_action: Open the Sections tab and look at the section names and entropy values. Does anything stand out as unusual?

glossary.definition: Portable Executable — the binary format for Windows programs.
The code section of a PE file.
A named region of a PE file with specific permissions (readable, writable, executable).
The address where execution begins when the program loads.

content.summary: ELF (Executable and Linkable Format) is the standard binary format for Linux, Android, and many Unix-like systems.
content.explanation: ELF files consist of an ELF header (magic bytes, architecture, entry point), program headers (segments loaded by the OS), and section headers (logical divisions like .text, .data, .bss). Unlike PE files, ELF uses a different security model: PIE (Position Independent Executable) is the ELF equivalent of ASLR, RELRO protects the GOT from overwrites, and the NX bit on the stack prevents shellcode execution. ELF binaries typically link against libc and other shared libraries via the GOT/PLT mechanism.
content.what_to_look_for: Check whether PIE, NX stack, and RELRO are enabled. Look at the interpreter path — unusual paths are a red flag. Check for stripped symbols, which hinder reverse engineering.
content.real_world_example: Mirai's ELF binaries for ARM and MIPS had all symbols stripped, imported mprotect and execve (for shellcode execution), and connected to a hardcoded C2 IP — all visible through static analysis.
content.next_action: Look at the Security tab for PIE/NX/RELRO status. Then check the Imports tab for suspicious libc functions.

glossary.definition: Executable and Linkable Format — the binary format for Linux and Unix-like systems.
Position Independent Executable — allows ASLR to randomise the base address.
Global Offset Table / Procedure Linkage Table — the mechanism ELF uses for calling shared library functions.
RELocation Read-Only — makes the GOT non-writable after linking, preventing GOT overwrites.

content.summary: Anya's risk score (0–100) is an additive indicator, not a verdict. A high score means many suspicious indicators were found; a low score means few indicators — not that the file is safe.
content.explanation: The risk score is calculated by summing weighted contributions: Critical findings add 25 points, High findings add 15, Medium add 8, and Low add 3. Structural findings like packer detection (+20), TLS callbacks (+10), and high-entropy overlay (+15) add additional weight. The score is capped at 100. Scores 0–29 are low risk, 30–59 medium, 60–79 high, and 80–100 critical. Important caveat: a score of 0 does not mean the file is safe — it means Anya found no indicators it was looking for. Novel malware, well-obfuscated files, and files that trigger nothing in the static signature set will score 0.
content.what_to_look_for: Look beyond the score at the individual findings. Two files with a score of 60 might be entirely different threats.
content.real_world_example: Stuxnet scored very high because it imported dozens of suspicious APIs, had a packed section, used TLS callbacks, and contained an encrypted payload overlay — every category contributed.
content.next_action: Read the detection tags below the score on the Overview tab. Each tag corresponds to a specific indicator found in this file.

glossary.definition: A 0–100 summary of how many and how severe the suspicious indicators found in a file are.
A legitimate file flagged as suspicious because it uses APIs or techniques also used by malware.
A malicious file that scores low because it uses techniques not in the signature set.

content.summary: Shannon entropy measures the randomness of data on a 0–8 scale. Packed or encrypted content has high entropy; normal code has moderate entropy.
content.explanation: Shannon entropy, developed by Claude Shannon in 1948, measures the average information content per byte in a sequence. For binary analysis, we measure it on a 0–8 scale (0 = completely uniform, 8 = perfectly random). Think of it like this: a dictionary page has low entropy because the same letters and words repeat constantly. White noise from a radio has high entropy because every sample is unpredictable. Compiled code has moderate entropy (4–6) because there are patterns in instruction sequences. Packed or encrypted data looks like random noise and measures 7.5–8.0. A section with entropy above 7.5 almost certainly contains encrypted or compressed data.
content.what_to_look_for: Open the Entropy tab. Look for sections or file-level entropy above 7.5. If the overall file entropy is high but there are few imports, the file is probably packed.
content.real_world_example: Every time Emotet updated its packing layer, the packed section entropy would be 7.9–8.0 — a consistent forensic indicator even when the hash changed daily.
content.next_action: Open the Entropy tab and note the file-level entropy value. Is it above 6.5? Above 7.5?

glossary.definition: A measure of the randomness or unpredictability of data, expressed on a 0–8 scale for binary files.
Compressing or encrypting a binary so its true content is only visible after the packer stub decrypts it in memory at runtime.

title: High Entropy Sections — What They Mean

content.summary: One or more sections in this file have unusually high entropy, suggesting encrypted or compressed content that hides the real code from static analysis.
content.explanation: High entropy in a PE section (above 7.0) almost always indicates one of three things: the section contains a compressed resource (like a PNG image), the section contains an encrypted payload that will be decrypted at runtime, or the file is packed and the section is the compressed original binary. The most suspicious scenario is a high-entropy section that is also marked executable — this is the classic packed malware pattern. A packer stub in a normal-entropy .text section decrypts the real code into a high-entropy section, then jumps to it.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at which specific section has high entropy. Is it executable? Does the entry point fall in that section? Are the imports suspiciously few (< 5 functions from 1–2 DLLs)?
content.real_world_example: IcedID (BokBot) consistently uses a high-entropy packed section with a small stub loader. The import table shows only 3–4 functions — far fewer than the actual capabilities the unpacked code would reveal.
content.next_action: Check the Sections tab. Look for sections marked with a high entropy warning. Note whether the entry point is in the first or a later section.

glossary.definition: A PE section with the IMAGE_SCN_MEM_EXECUTE flag, meaning the CPU can run code from it.
A binary where the original code is compressed or encrypted and a stub loader unpacks it at runtime.
Anya flags sections as suspicious when Shannon entropy exceeds 7.5.

content.summary: This file appears to be packed. Static analysis of packed files is limited — the real import table and code are only visible after unpacking.
content.explanation: A packed binary has been compressed or encrypted so that the original code is hidden. What you see in Anya's import table is just the packer stub's imports — a tiny fraction of what the unpacked binary would reveal. The typical static analysis limitation: you can confirm packing, identify the packer (if it's a known one like UPX), and note the entropy of sections, but you cannot see the real API calls until the binary is unpacked. For UPX-packed files, unpacking is trivial: use 'upx -d filename.exe'. For custom packers, you'll need dynamic analysis (run in a sandbox or unpack manually in a debugger).
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the packer detection result in the Overview tab. Check section names for packer signatures (UPX0/UPX1, .vmp0, .themida). Note the import count — packed files usually import fewer than 5 functions.
content.real_world_example: The Mirai botnet spread as UPX-packed ELF binaries. Unpacking with 'upx -d' immediately revealed the full import table including connect, socket, and send — the C2 communication functions.
content.next_action: If this is a UPX-packed file, try: upx -d suspicious.exe (Linux/macOS) or use a tool like CFF Explorer on Windows. Then re-analyse the unpacked file in Anya.

glossary.definition: Ultimate Packer for eXecutables — a free, open-source executable packer widely used by both legitimate software and malware.
The unpacking code that runs first in a packed binary, decrypts the real payload, and jumps to it.
After unpacking, many more imports become visible that were hidden by the packer.

content.summary: The import table lists every external function a program calls. It's the single most revealing piece of information in static analysis.
content.explanation: Windows programs don't contain all the code they use. Instead, they import functions from Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs) — shared libraries that Windows provides. The import table lists every DLL and every function the program calls from that DLL. This directly reveals capabilities: if a program imports CreateRemoteThread, it can inject code into other processes. If it imports InternetOpen, it can make HTTP requests. If it imports SetWindowsHookEx, it can intercept all keystrokes. The import table is the single most revealing artefact in static analysis — more so than the code itself for quick triage.
content.what_to_look_for: Open the Imports tab. Look for functions grouped under kernel32.dll (low-level process/memory operations), ntdll.dll (direct NT API — more suspicious), wininet.dll or winhttp.dll (networking), and advapi32.dll (registry/services/tokens).
content.real_world_example: When analysts saw that a suspicious file imported OpenProcess + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread + VirtualAllocEx together, they immediately classified it as a process injector before reading a single instruction of assembly.
content.next_action: Open the Imports tab. Are there any red-highlighted suspicious APIs? Click on a suspicious API badge to see which MITRE ATT&CK technique it maps to.

glossary.definition: Dynamic Link Library — a shared library on Windows that multiple programs can use simultaneously.
Import Address Table — the in-memory version of the import table, filled in by the Windows loader.
An API reference by number instead of name, used to hide the function being called.

title: Process Injection APIs — Step by Step

content.summary: This file contains the three-API process injection combo: VirtualAllocEx + WriteProcessMemory + CreateRemoteThread.
content.explanation: Classic process injection works in three steps: (1) VirtualAllocEx — allocate a region of memory inside a *different* running process, specifying PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE to make it both writable and executable. (2) WriteProcessMemory — copy shellcode or a DLL path into that allocated region. (3) CreateRemoteThread — create a thread inside the target process, with the start address pointing to the code just written. The result: your code runs inside a legitimate process like explorer.exe or svchost.exe, inheriting its network permissions and evading process-based detection. No file is written to disk for the payload itself.
content.what_to_look_for: Look for a target process hint in the Strings tab — malware often hardcodes 'explorer.exe', 'svchost.exe', or 'lsass.exe' as injection targets. Also check for OpenProcess (needed before VirtualAllocEx) in the import table.
content.real_world_example: Cobalt Strike's default process injection technique uses exactly this combo, often targeting notepad.exe or svchost.exe. The process can be identified by looking for CreateToolhelp32Snapshot (process enumeration to find the target) alongside the injection trio.
content.next_action: Check the Strings tab for hardcoded process names. Look for CreateToolhelp32Snapshot — used to enumerate running processes to find the injection target.

glossary.definition: Executing code inside another process's address space to evade detection and inherit permissions.
A memory protection flag making a region both writable and executable — very unusual in legitimate code.
A thread created inside a different process, as opposed to within the same process.

content.summary: This file imports some functions by number (ordinal) rather than by name, hiding which APIs it calls from basic static analysis tools.
content.explanation: Normally, the import table lists function names like 'CreateRemoteThread'. With ordinal imports, instead of a name, you see only a number — e.g., ntdll.dll ordinal #12. To know which function ordinal #12 maps to, you need to look up the export table of ntdll.dll from the exact Windows version the malware targets. This is a deliberate evasion technique: signature-based tools that scan for API names will miss ordinal-imported functions entirely.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at which DLL has ordinal imports. Ordinals from ntdll.dll or kernel32.dll are more concerning than from user32.dll or shell32.dll.
content.real_world_example: Some variants of PlugX and Turla used ntdll ordinal imports for their process injection routines specifically to bypass string-based AV signatures that looked for 'NtCreateThreadEx' in the import table.
content.next_action: Note the DLL name and ordinal number. To resolve it, download the matching DLL from a trusted Windows installation and use a tool like Ghidra, PE-bear, or CFF Explorer to view its exports.

glossary.definition: An import referenced by number rather than by name.
The numeric identifier for a function in a DLL's export table.

content.summary: Different DLLs reveal different capabilities. Knowing what each DLL is used for lets you quickly assess what a binary can do.
content.explanation: Key DLLs and what they reveal: kernel32.dll — core process, memory, file, and thread operations. ntdll.dll — direct NT kernel API (more powerful and lower-level than kernel32; ntdll imports are more suspicious because they can bypass user-mode hooks). wininet.dll / winhttp.dll — HTTP/HTTPS networking. ws2_32.dll — raw TCP/UDP sockets. advapi32.dll — registry, services, cryptography, security tokens. shell32.dll — shell operations, file associations. psapi.dll / toolhelp32 — process enumeration (reconnaissance). A file importing only kernel32.dll + ntdll.dll with no other DLLs may be trying to minimise its detectable surface.
content.what_to_look_for: Count the DLLs. Legitimate applications typically import from 5–15 DLLs. Fewer than 3 (especially in an apparently complex program) suggests packing or ordinal-only imports.
content.real_world_example: Early Remcos RAT samples imported only from kernel32.dll, ntdll.dll, and ws2_32.dll — three DLLs giving it full process control plus raw socket networking, with a minimal import footprint.
content.next_action: Look at the DLL list in the Imports tab. Are there any unexpected DLLs? Is the DLL count unusually low?

glossary.definition: The NT layer DLL — provides direct access to Windows NT system calls, bypassing higher-level user-mode hooks.
The Windows Sockets 2 DLL — used for TCP/UDP socket networking.

content.summary: This file calls APIs to check whether it is being run inside a debugger, a common anti-analysis technique.
content.explanation: The most common approach is IsDebuggerPresent — a single API that returns TRUE if the calling process is being debugged. Malware typically calls this early and exits cleanly (or loads a decoy) if it returns TRUE. CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent can detect debuggers attached from another process. NtQueryInformationProcess with ProcessDebugPort is a lower-level version that bypasses some patching. More sophisticated techniques check the BeingDebugged flag in the PEB directly (without an API call), use timing checks (execution under a debugger is slower), and check for hardware breakpoints via debug registers.
content.what_to_look_for: Look for IsDebuggerPresent AND a conditional early-exit pattern (usually followed by ExitProcess or a clean-looking return). Also check if there are timing APIs (GetTickCount, QueryPerformanceCounter) nearby — timing anti-debug is often combined with API-based checks.
content.real_world_example: Banking trojan Dridex patched its own IsDebuggerPresent to always return FALSE — it detected and patched the analysis tool check before the analyst could patch it back. Only hardware debugger detection (checking debug registers) could bypass this.
content.next_action: Note the full set of anti-analysis indicators in the Overview tab. Is this the only check, or are there VM checks and timing checks as well? Multiple methods suggest a more sophisticated evasion strategy.

glossary.definition: Windows API that returns TRUE if the current process has a debugger attached.
Process Environment Block — an in-memory structure containing process state, including a BeingDebugged flag.
A popular plugin for x64dbg that patches out common debugger detection techniques.

content.summary: This file may attempt to detect whether it is running inside a virtual machine or analysis sandbox, a technique used to evade automated analysis.
content.explanation: VMs and sandboxes leave many artefacts: registry keys (HKLM\SOFTWARE\VMware, HKLM\SOFTWARE\VBOX), process names (vmwaretray.exe, vboxservice.exe), MAC address prefixes (00:0C:29 for VMware, 08:00:27 for VirtualBox), CPUID leaf 0x40000000 (hypervisor vendor string), unusual disk names, and low uptime. The GetSystemFirmwareTable API retrieves ACPI/SMBIOS data — VM-hosted firmware has distinctive strings (VMware, VBOX, QEMU). Malware that detects it's in a sandbox typically terminates, sleeps for a very long time, or executes a benign code path.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the Strings tab for VBOX, VMWARE, QEMU, or SANDBOX strings. Also check the anti-analysis indicators section in the Overview tab.
content.real_world_example: Emotet stopped executing in environments where the computer name was 'DESKTOP-' followed by a standard pattern, where the username was 'admin' or 'analyst', or where there were fewer than 100 files in common directories — all sandbox indicators.
content.next_action: Run this file in a heavily customised sandbox (non-standard username, realistic file count, physical hardware MAC addresses) or on bare metal in an isolated environment.

glossary.definition: A CPU instruction that returns processor and hypervisor identification — detectable from software.
System Management BIOS — firmware tables that contain hardware identifiers, often with VM-specific values.
A real physical machine as opposed to a virtual machine — harder for malware to detect.

content.summary: This file has TLS (Thread Local Storage) callbacks — functions that execute before the program's entry point, before most debuggers can intercept them.
content.explanation: Thread Local Storage callbacks are executed by the Windows loader before calling the program's entry point (main/WinMain). This means: before most debuggers attach at 'entry point', before many sandbox hooks are in place, and before the analyst's first breakpoint fires. Malware uses TLS callbacks for: checking for debuggers and exiting before analysis begins, decrypting the real payload that is then executed at the normal entry point, unpacking the import table, and running anti-analysis code that modifies the binary in memory before it appears to start.
content.what_to_look_for: Note the callback addresses shown in Anya's JSON output. In x64dbg, set a breakpoint on every TLS callback VirtualAddress before running the file. The entry point you see in the Overview tab is where *main* starts, not where TLS callbacks start.
content.real_world_example: Some Delphi-compiled malware families use TLS callbacks as their primary anti-debug mechanism — the real IsDebuggerPresent check happens in TLS, long before the debugger-visible entry point is reached.
content.next_action: In x64dbg: Options → Preferences → Events → check 'TLS Callbacks'. This ensures x64dbg breaks at every TLS callback before entry.

glossary.definition: Thread Local Storage — a mechanism for per-thread data, whose callbacks are called before program entry.
A function registered in the TLS directory that Windows calls before the program's main entry point.
A popular open-source debugger for 64-bit Windows binaries.

title: Data After the Last Section — Overlay

content.summary: This file has data appended after its last PE section — a pattern used by droppers to hide encrypted payloads from section-based analysis.
content.explanation: The PE format only defines sections up to a certain file offset. Any bytes after the last section's raw data end are called an 'overlay' and are ignored by the Windows loader. Legitimate software does use overlays: self-extracting archives (WinRAR/7-Zip), digitally signed PE files (the signature sits in an overlay), and some installers. For malware, overlays are a convenient place to store an encrypted second-stage payload, configuration data (C2 addresses, encryption keys), or an embedded file to drop to disk. The overlay's entropy indicates whether the data is encrypted (high entropy) or plaintext (low entropy).
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the overlay entropy in Anya's JSON output. High entropy (> 6.5) suggests encrypted content. Note the offset and size — you can extract it with: dd if=suspicious.exe bs=1 skip=OFFSET count=SIZE of=overlay.bin
content.real_world_example: Many Dridex samples carried their encrypted configuration blob as a 4–8 KB overlay at the end of an otherwise legitimate-looking PE. The overlay entropy was consistently 7.9–8.0.
content.next_action: If overlay entropy is high: extract the overlay bytes using the offset/size from Anya's JSON output. Try to determine the file type (check for magic bytes), look for repeated patterns suggesting an XOR key, and submit to a sandbox.

glossary.definition: Data appended after the last PE section — ignored by the Windows loader but accessible to the program via file I/O.
The first few bytes of a file that identify its format (e.g., 'MZ' for PE, 'PK' for ZIP).

title: ASLR and DEP — Why They Matter

content.summary: ASLR and DEP are security mitigations that make exploiting memory corruption bugs significantly harder. Their absence is a suspicious indicator.
content.explanation: ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomisation) randomises where code, stack, and heap are loaded in memory, making it harder for exploit code to jump to a known address. In PE files, ASLR is enabled by the IMAGE_DLLCHARACTERISTICS_DYNAMIC_BASE flag in the optional header. DEP (Data Execution Prevention) / NX marks data pages as non-executable, preventing shellcode from running in stack or heap memory. Enabled with IMAGE_DLLCHARACTERISTICS_NX_COMPAT. Legitimate software compiled with modern toolchains (MSVC, GCC, Clang) enables both by default. A PE without these flags was either compiled with a very old toolchain, deliberately stripped of them to improve exploit reliability, or is a handcrafted binary.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the Security tab. Note which flags are disabled. A PE missing both ASLR and DEP is an immediate red flag unless it's clearly a very old legacy binary.
content.real_world_example: Early Wannacry samples had DEP disabled — a relic of the exploit code's origin in the EternalBlue exploit that assumed an NX-disabled environment.
content.next_action: Check the Security tab. If both ASLR and DEP are disabled, combine this with the overall risk score for assessment.

glossary.definition: Address Space Layout Randomisation — randomises the load address of code and data to hinder exploitation.
Data Execution Prevention / No-eXecute — prevents code from running in data memory regions.
A field in the PE optional header that encodes security feature flags.

content.summary: MITRE ATT&CK is a globally-accessible knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) observed in real-world attacks.
content.explanation: MITRE ATT&CK organises adversary behaviour into 14 Tactics (high-level goals like Persistence, Privilege Escalation, Defense Evasion) and hundreds of Techniques and Sub-techniques (specific methods). Each technique has a unique ID (e.g., T1055.001 for Process Injection: Dynamic-link Library Injection) with documented procedure examples, mitigations, and detection guidance. When Anya identifies an API like CreateRemoteThread, it maps it to T1055.001 — immediately placing the finding in the context of real-world threat intelligence. SOC analysts use ATT&CK IDs in incident reports to communicate precisely what behaviour was observed.
content.what_to_look_for: Look at the technique IDs in the Imports tab badges and the MITRE section of the JSON output. Click a badge to open the ATT&CK website page for that technique.
content.real_world_example: When APT29 (Cozy Bear) was tracked, security researchers used ATT&CK to map their observed TTPs: T1055 (Process Injection), T1547.001 (Registry Run Keys), T1003.001 (LSASS Dumping). The shared vocabulary allowed teams worldwide to compare findings.
content.next_action: Click on an ATT&CK badge in the Imports tab to open the ATT&CK website page for that technique. Look at the 'Procedure Examples' section — it lists real malware families that use that technique.

glossary.definition: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques used in real cyberattacks, maintained by MITRE Corporation.
Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures — the ways adversaries operate.
A specific implementation of a broader technique, identified by a decimal suffix (e.g., T1055.001).

content.summary: Each ATT&CK technique page is a wealth of threat intelligence — it lists real malware families, detection methods, and mitigations.
content.explanation: An ATT&CK technique page has several sections: Description (what the technique does and why), Procedure Examples (real malware families and how they used it — with citations to threat intelligence reports), Mitigations (how to prevent it), Detection (what to look for in logs and artefacts), and References (links to the research reports). The Procedure Examples section is especially valuable for matching what you found in the file to known threat actors.
content.what_to_look_for: Click a badge in Anya's Imports tab. On the ATT&CK page, go to 'Procedure Examples'. Do any named threat actors or malware families match what you know about the suspected source of this file?
content.real_world_example: T1003.001 (LSASS Memory) lists Mimikatz as a procedure example with a citation to the original research. If you find MinidumpWriteDump in a sample, you can immediately check which other groups have used the same technique.
content.next_action: Visit the ATT&CK technique page for the highest-confidence technique found in this file. Read the Procedure Examples and note if any described malware families match this sample's other indicators.

glossary.definition: A documented real-world use of an ATT&CK technique by a specific threat actor or malware family.
An individual, group, or nation-state conducting cyberattacks.

content.summary: A structured workflow for triaging a suspicious file from first look to escalation decision.
content.explanation: The standard triage sequence: (1) Hashes — check MD5/SHA256 on MalwareBazaar, VirusTotal, and your threat intelligence platform. Known-bad hash = instant escalation. (2) Entropy — is it packed? If entropy > 7.5, unpacking is needed before meaningful analysis. (3) Imports — what can it do? Look for injection, persistence, network, and anti-analysis APIs. (4) Strings — what did the developer leave behind? IP addresses, domains, registry paths, file paths, error messages, and PDB paths are all useful. (5) Security flags — does it have ASLR/DEP? (6) Risk score + MITRE techniques — what is the overall assessment? (7) Escalate if risk score > 60, or if any Critical-confidence finding is present.
content.what_to_look_for: Work through Anya's tabs in order: Overview (hashes, score) → Entropy → Imports → Strings → Security → Export the JSON for your ticket.
content.real_world_example: SOC analysts at a financial institution used exactly this workflow to triage 47 suspicious email attachments in 4 hours during a targeted phishing campaign, correctly identifying 3 as novel banking trojans and 44 as known commodity loaders.
content.next_action: Use Anya's JSON export to create a triage summary. Include: file hash, risk score, top 3 suspicious imports, MITRE technique IDs, and your escalation recommendation.

glossary.definition: The process of quickly assessing a suspicious file to determine its priority and next action.
A free public repository of malware samples and hashes maintained by abuse.ch.
A path to a debugging symbols file embedded in the PE — often reveals the attacker's development machine directory structure.

content.summary: Knowing when to escalate from triage to full incident response is a critical analyst skill. Here are the thresholds.
content.explanation: Escalate immediately (to IR team) if: any Critical-confidence finding is present (process injection combo, LSASS dump capability), risk score > 70, novel malware (no hash match on any threat intelligence platform), evidence of targeting (custom implant, PDB path with internal project names, strings in the target's language), or active infection indicators (the file was found running or established persistence). Continue static analysis if: medium-confidence findings only, known commodity malware (hash match found), no evidence of execution. Move to dynamic analysis (sandbox) if: file is packed (static analysis is limited), anti-analysis techniques detected, need to observe network communication or dropped files.
content.what_to_look_for: Review the MITRE techniques and confidence scores in the JSON output. Any Critical finding → escalate. Risk score > 70 without a known-good explanation → escalate.
content.real_world_example: A SOC analyst found a file with risk score 85, MinidumpWriteDump (Critical), and a PDB path containing 'lsass_dump_tool' — immediate IR escalation led to finding 12 compromised machines with credential theft activity.
content.next_action: Write a one-paragraph triage summary: 'This file is a [PE/ELF], risk score [N]. It imports [top 3 suspicious APIs] suggesting [capability]. MITRE techniques: [IDs]. Confidence: [level]. Recommendation: [action].'

glossary.definition: Incident Response — the structured process of detecting, containing, and recovering from a security incident.
A previously unseen malware sample with no hash match in any threat intelligence database.
Indicator of Compromise — an artefact (hash, IP, domain, file path) that indicates a system has been compromised.
